description: This bar chart presents the austenite volume fraction (vol.%) measured by XRD in an Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C low-density duplex steel before and after tensile deformation, comparing six different processing conditions. The x-axis labels denote the cooling path and annealing temperature: AC (air-cooled) and FC (furnace-cooled) samples annealed at 800, 900, and 1000 °C for 30 min. Red bars represent pre-deformation fractions, while hatched gray bars show post-deformation fractions. Key observations include: (1) Air-cooled samples exhibit substantial austenite consumption during deformation, with AC800 dropping from 58.6% to 23.7%, AC900 from 61.4% to 44.4%, and AC1000 from 57.1% to 46.4%, indicating significant transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP) effect. (2) Furnace-cooled samples retain most of their austenite after deformation, with FC800 decreasing only from 62% to 56.5%, FC900 from 58.6% to 52.4%, and FC1000 from 60.6% to 52.1%, attributed to higher chemical stabilization and larger grain sizes. (3) FC800 contains the highest initial austenite fraction (62%), whereas AC800 shows the lowest retained austenite after deformation (23.7%). The figure supports the conclusion that the TRIP effect contributes to work hardening rate recovery in air-cooled annealed samples, while the presence of martensite in furnace-cooled samples promotes crack nucleation at martensite-austenite interfaces, leading to brittle fracture without necking.
